reasoning:
The condition requires strictly more than 3.5 'Riots' (i.e. 4 or more) within the 30-day period leading up to each resolution date. The 3.5 baseline indicates that 42 riots occurred over the previous 360-day window. Because civil unrest data is heavily clustered and overdispersed, the mean (3.5) is typically significantly higher than the median month. This suggests that a randomly selected month will usually have fewer than 3.5 riots, with occasional spikes driving up the average. Furthermore, February and March are cold winter months, historically associated with a lower frequency of street protests and mob violence compared to summer. 

For the first resolution (Feb 22), 23 of the 30 days have already passed (Jan 23 - Feb 15). Extensive news searches for targeted keywords (riots, clashes, petrol bombs, scuffles) in UK and NI regional media over this timeframe yielded zero prominent reports. Reaching 4 riots in just the remaining 7 days without an already established baseline of unrest is highly unlikely. Thus, the probability is very low. 

For the second resolution (March 17), the 30-day evaluation window begins today. Our uncertainty inherently increases, returning the estimate closer to the general base rate of any given winter/early-spring month exceeding the annual average. Factoring in the typical winter suppression, I estimate this at roughly 25%.
